turns.svg:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="512" height="512" viewBox="0 0 512 512">
  <!-- background -->
  <rect x="0" y="0" width="512" height="512" fill="#ffffff"/>

  <!-- floor/ground -->
  <path d="M60 420 C140 385, 210 380, 260 388 C330 400, 390 398, 452 372 L452 510 L60 510 Z" fill="#eef2f7"/>

  <!-- desk -->
  <rect x="110" y="300" width="292" height="160" rx="18" fill="#c8a47a" stroke="#8b6a44" stroke-width="4"/>
  <rect x="90" y="280" width="332" height="40" rx="14" fill="#b99063" stroke="#7a5b37" stroke-width="4"/>

  <!-- desk shadow -->
  <ellipse cx="256" cy="432" rx="150" ry="28" fill="#000" opacity="0.10"/>

  <!-- open book -->
  <path d="M150 285 C190 250, 230 250, 256 260 C282 250, 322 250, 362 285 L362 330 C322 300, 282 300, 256 315 C230 300, 190 300, 150 330 Z" fill="#f6f0e6" stroke="#3c2f2a" stroke-width="4"/>
  <!-- book spine -->
  <path d="M256 260 C256 285, 256 325, 256 315" stroke="#3c2f2a" stroke-width="6" fill="none"/>
  <!-- pages folds/lines -->
  <path d="M180 292 C210 278, 235 276, 245 282" stroke="#3c2f2a" stroke-width="3" fill="none" opacity="0.5"/>
  <path d="M210 320 C235 310, 245 310, 252 315" stroke="#3c2f2a" stroke-width="3" fill="none" opacity="0.5"/>
  <path d="M332 292 C302 278, 277 276, 267 282" stroke="#3c2f2a" stroke-width="3" fill="none" opacity="0.5"/>
  <path d="M302 320 C277 310, 267 310, 260 315" stroke="#3c2f2a" stroke-width="3" fill="none" opacity="0.5"/>

  <!-- pencil -->
  <g transform="translate(0,0)" opacity="1">
    <rect x="170" y="245" width="170" height="18" rx="9" fill="#f0c24b" stroke="#6b4d1f" stroke-width="3"/>
    <polygon points="340,253 360,244 360,262 340,271" fill="#d94f3d" stroke="#6b2d28" stroke-width="3"/>
    <polygon points="170,245 190,236 190,254 170,263" fill="#cbd6e2" stroke="#52606d" stroke-width="3"/>
    <rect x="184" y="247" width="10" height="14" rx="5" fill="#c27" opacity="0.0"/>
  </g>

  <!-- bookmark/strap (small) -->
  <path d="M230 300 L244 292 L244 352 L230 360 Z" fill="#7a3cff" opacity="0.25"/>

  <!-- subtle highlights -->
  <path d="M128 308 C170 278, 214 278, 250 288" stroke="#fff" stroke-width="6" opacity="0.25" fill="none"/>

  <!-- magnifying glass for studying -->
  <g transform="translate(0,0)">
    <circle cx="380" cy="220" r="44" fill="#d6e7ff" stroke="#3a5a8a" stroke-width="5"/>
    <circle cx="380" cy="220" r="28" fill="#9bd1ff" opacity="0.20" stroke="#1f3f66" stroke-width="4"/>
    <path d="M410 252 L465 307" stroke="#2f3a4a" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/>
    <path d="M410 252 L465 307" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round" opacity="0.7"/>
  </g>
</svg>

messages.content:
You are a drawing agent in a one-word guessing game.
Return JSON only, with a single field: svg.

Rules:
1) Draw the target word using only SVG graphics.
2) Output one complete <svg>...</svg> string.
3) Do not include text labels, letters, or numbers in the drawing.
4) Keep the SVG simple and valid. No scripts, no foreignObject.
5) Use a 512x512 canvas.

Output schema:
{ "svg": "string" }

message.reasoning:
**Creating drawing concept**

I need to create a one-word guessing game drawing that doesn’t include any text labels. The idea is to draw a study scene featuring elements like a book, a desk, a pencil, and possibly a magnifying glass. It has to be simple SVG art, so I’ll focus on using shapes to depict a desk with an open book and a pencil. Finally, I’ll return this as a JSON containing the SVG string.

messages.content:
You are the guesser in a visual one-word guessing game.
Return JSON only, with a single field: guesses.

Rules:
1) You receive one JPEG image of a drawing.
2) Return exactly 20 one-word guesses in order of confidence (best guess first).
3) The order is important and will be scored by first correct position.
4) Do not repeat guesses.
5) Each guess must be EN-US one word, no spaces.
6) Avoid punctuation and numbers.

Output schema:
{ "guesses": ["string", "... exactly 20 items ..."] }

content.text: {"language":"EN-US","maxHints":20,"note":"Return 20 ordered guesses. Position 1 is the highest-confidence guess."}
